key_points: Force is a push or pull that can change the state of motion or shape of an object
Balanced forces cancel each other and do not change the state of motion
Unbalanced forces result in acceleration or change in direction of motion
Inertia is the resistance offered by an object to change its state of rest or motion
The inertia of an object increases with its mass
Newton’s First Law explains inertia and states that objects remain in their state unless acted upon by an external force
Momentum is the quantity of motion possessed by a moving body
Momentum depends on both mass and velocity of the object
Newton’s Second Law gives the quantitative relationship between force, mass and acceleration
Newton’s Third Law explains action and reaction forces acting on different bodies
examples: A book lying on a table experiences balanced forces
A football moves when kicked due to unbalanced force
Passengers fall forward when a bus stops suddenly due to inertia of motion
Passengers move backward when a bus starts suddenly due to inertia of rest
Recoil of a gun demonstrates Newton’s Third Law
Rocket propulsion works on the principle of action and reaction
exam_tips: Remember that Newton’s First Law is also called the Law of Inertia
Mass is a measure of inertia
Momentum increases with increase in velocity or mass
Action and reaction forces are equal and opposite
Force and momentum are vector quantities
